docstring_spec:
Given a list `items` of hashable elements, return a new list containing only
the first occurrence of each distinct element, preserving the original
relative order (a "stable" dedup). Elements are compared by equality/hash
(works for ints, strings, tuples, etc.). The input list is never mutated.
Edge cases: an empty list returns an empty list; a list where every element
is identical collapses to a single-element list; a list with all-unique
elements returns an equal copy in the same order; non-adjacent duplicates
(e.g. an element reappearing much later) are still removed, keeping only the
first occurrence.

Given a list `items` of comparable values, return a run-length encoding as a
list of `[value, count]` pairs (each a 2-element list), where each pair
represents one maximal run of consecutive equal values in the original
order. Runs are determined purely by adjacency: if the same value appears
again later but non-adjacently, it starts a new run rather than merging with
an earlier one. Edge cases: an empty list encodes to an empty list; a
single-element list encodes to one pair with count 1; a list of all-equal
elements encodes to a single pair; concatenating the counts of the result
always equals the length of the input.

Split `items` into consecutive chunks of size `n` and return the list of
chunks (each a list). `n` must be a positive integer; if `n <= 0`, raise
ValueError. Chunks are taken in order from the front; the last chunk may
contain fewer than `n` elements if `len(items)` is not evenly divisible by
`n`. Edge cases: an empty `items` list returns an empty list of chunks
regardless of `n`; if `n` is larger than `len(items)`, the result is a
single chunk containing all elements; concatenating all returned chunks in
order reproduces the original list exactly.

Given a list of ints `values` and an int `k`, return the indices (into
`values`) of the `k` largest values, as a list sorted in ascending index
order. Selection uses stable tie-breaking: among equal values, elements with
the smaller original index are preferred (equivalent to sorting candidates
by `(-value, index)` and taking the first `k`); the returned list is then
re-sorted ascending by index (not by value) so callers can read off
positions left-to-right. Edge cases: `k <= 0` or an empty `values` returns
`[]`; if `k >= len(values)`, the result is `list(range(len(values)))` (every
index, ascending); negative values are handled the same as positive ones.

Given a directed graph `graph` as an adjacency dict mapping node -> list of
neighbor nodes (a node absent as a key has no outgoing edges), and nodes
`start` and `end`, return True if there is a directed path from `start` to
`end`, else False. A zero-length path counts: if `start == end`, the result
is True even if `start` has no entry in `graph` at all. The traversal must
tolerate cycles without looping forever (track visited nodes). Edge cases:
`start == end` on an empty graph is True; a node with no outgoing edges can
only reach itself; a two-node cycle correctly reports reachability between
its members and correctly reports unreachability to a node outside the
cycle; a self-loop counts as `start == end` trivially true regardless of the
edge.
hidden_tests:
def check(fn):
    assert fn({}, 'a', 'a') is True
    assert fn({'a': ['b']}, 'a', 'a') is True
    assert fn({'a': ['b'], 'b': ['c'], 'c': []}, 'a', 'c') is True
    assert fn({'a': ['b'], 'b': ['c'], 'c': []}, 'a', 'd') is False
    assert fn({'a': ['b'], 'b': ['a']}, 'a', 'b') is True
    assert fn({'a': ['b'], 'b': ['a']}, 'a', 'c') is False
    assert fn({'a': [], 'b': ['a']}, 'b', 'a') is True
    assert fn({'a': []}, 'a', 'b') is False
    assert fn({'a': ['a']}, 'a', 'a') is True
    assert fn({'a': []}, 'b', 'a') is False
    assert fn({'a': ['z']}, 'a', 'b') is False

docstring_spec:
Given a directed graph `graph` as an adjacency dict mapping node -> list of
neighbor nodes (a neighbor may be a node with no key of its own, meaning no
further outgoing edges), determine whether the graph contains a directed
cycle. Return True if a cycle exists, False if the graph is a DAG. The
implementation must use proper three-color (white/gray/black) DFS so that a
node revisited via a *different* path after it has already finished
(a "cross edge" in a DAG, e.g. a diamond shape where two branches rejoin at
a shared descendant) is NOT mistaken for a cycle -- only a back-edge to a
node still on the current recursion stack counts. Edge cases: an empty
graph is not cyclic; a single node with no self-edge is not cyclic; a
self-loop (`'a': ['a']`) is cyclic; a diamond DAG (two parents sharing one
child) is not cyclic even though the shared child is visited twice.
hidden_tests:
def check(fn):
    assert fn({}) is False
    assert fn({'a': []}) is False
    assert fn({'a': ['a']}) is True
    assert fn({'a': ['b'], 'b': ['c'], 'c': []}) is False
    assert fn({'a': ['b'], 'b': ['c'], 'c': ['a']}) is True
    assert fn({'a': ['b', 'c'], 'b': ['d'], 'c': ['d'], 'd': []}) is False
    assert fn({'a': ['b'], 'b': ['a'], 'c': []}) is True
    assert fn({'x': ['y'], 'y': []}) is False
    assert fn({'a': ['b'], 'c': ['a'], 'b': ['c']}) is True

docstring_spec:
Given a tree encoded as a parent map `parent`: a dict mapping each
non-root child node to its parent node (root nodes are exactly the nodes
that never appear as a key in `parent`), return a dict mapping every node
that appears anywhere in `parent` (as a key or as a value) to its depth,
where a root has depth 0 and any other node has depth `1 +
depth(parent[node])`. The input is assumed to be a valid, finite, acyclic
forest. Edge cases: an empty `parent` map returns `{}`; two children sharing
one root both get depth 1; a straight chain of depth 3 assigns depths
0,1,2,3 correctly; a branching tree assigns each node the depth along its
own path from its root, independent of siblings' depths.

Given a tree encoded as a parent map `parent` using the same convention as
`tree_depth` (child -> parent; root nodes never appear as a key), return a
dict mapping every node appearing anywhere in `parent` to the size of its
subtree: 1 (for itself) plus the total number of its descendants at any
depth. Leaves (nodes with no children) always have subtree size 1. Edge
cases: an empty `parent` map returns `{}`; a root's subtree size always
equals the total number of nodes in the tree; a two-level chain gives the
root size 3, the middle node size 2, and the leaf size 1; a node with
multiple children sums all of their subtree sizes plus one for itself.

Each axis-aligned rectangle is given as a tuple `(x1, y1, x2, y2)` with
`x1 < x2` and `y1 < y2` (lower-left and upper-right integer corners). Given
two rectangles `a` and `b`, return the integer area of their overlapping
region. If the rectangles do not overlap -- including the case where they
only touch along an edge or at a single corner, giving zero width or zero
height -- return 0. Edge cases: rectangles that merely touch (share a
boundary line or a single point) overlap with area 0, not a positive area;
one rectangle fully containing another yields the area of the smaller one;
two identical rectangles yield the full area of either; rectangles far
apart with no shared coordinates return 0; negative coordinates are
supported.
reference_solution:
def rect_overlap_area(a, b):
    ax1, ay1, ax2, ay2 = a
    bx1, by1, bx2, by2 = b
    ox1 = max(ax1, bx1)
    oy1 = max(ay1, by1)
    ox2 = min(ax2, bx2)
    oy2 = min(ay2, by2)
    w = ox2 - ox1
    h = oy2 - oy1
    if w <= 0 or h <= 0:
        return 0
    return w * h

docstring_spec:
Given a list `path` of integer 2D points (each a tuple `(x, y)`) visited in
order, return the total Manhattan distance traveled: the sum, over each
consecutive pair of points, of `|x2 - x1| + |y2 - y1|`. Edge cases: a path
with 0 or 1 points has traveled distance 0 (no movement possible); a
segment between two identical consecutive points contributes 0; a path can
revisit the same point many times in a row without changing the running
total; negative coordinates are handled the same as positive ones.
reference_solution:
def manhattan_path_length(path):
    total = 0
    for i in range(1, len(path)):
        x1, y1 = path[i - 1]
        x2, y2 = path[i]
        total += abs(x2 - x1) + abs(y2 - y1)
    return total

docstring_spec:
Given a point `(x, y)` and an axis-aligned rectangle `(x1, y1, x2, y2)` with
`x1 <= x2` and `y1 <= y2`, return True if the point lies inside the
rectangle OR exactly on its boundary (inclusive on all four sides), else
False. Edge cases: a point on any single edge (left/right/top/bottom) is
True; a point exactly on a corner is True; a point just outside any one
side is False even if it matches the other axis; negative-coordinate
rectangles are supported the same way as positive ones.
reference_solution:
def point_in_rect(point, rect):
    x, y = point
    x1, y1, x2, y2 = rect
    return x1 <= x <= x2 and y1 <= y <= y2
hidden_tests:
def check(fn):
    assert fn((1, 1), (0, 0, 2, 2)) is True
    assert fn((0, 0), (0, 0, 2, 2)) is True
    assert fn((2, 2), (0, 0, 2, 2)) is True
    assert fn((0, 1), (0, 0, 2, 2)) is True
    assert fn((3, 1), (0, 0, 2, 2)) is False
    assert fn((-1, 1), (0, 0, 2, 2)) is False
    assert fn((1, -1), (0, 0, 2, 2)) is False
    assert fn((1, 3), (0, 0, 2, 2)) is False
    assert fn((-5, -5), (-10, -10, -1, -1)) is True

docstring_spec:
Given a non-empty list `points` of integer 2D points (tuples `(x, y)`),
return the minimal axis-aligned bounding box containing all of them, as a
tuple `(minx, miny, maxx, maxy)`. If `points` is empty, raise ValueError.
Edge cases: a single point yields a zero-area box `(x, y, x, y)`; repeated
identical points yield the same degenerate box; points where every x
coordinate is equal still produce correct min/max on y (and vice versa);
negative coordinates are handled correctly.

Given a non-empty list of ints `values`, return the lower median: sort the
values ascending into `s` of length `n`; if `n` is odd, return
`s[n // 2]`; if `n` is even, return `s[n // 2 - 1]` (the smaller of the two
middle values, NOT an average). Raise ValueError if `values` is empty.
Edge cases: a single-element list returns that element; an even-length list
returns the lower, not the upper, of the two middle values; unsorted input
is handled correctly (the function sorts internally); duplicate values are
handled like any other value.
hidden_tests:
def check(fn):
    assert fn([5]) == 5
    assert fn([1, 2, 3]) == 2
    assert fn([1, 2, 3, 4]) == 2
    assert fn([4, 3, 2, 1]) == 2
    assert fn([1, 1, 1, 1]) == 1
    assert fn([-5, -1, 0, 3]) == -1
    assert fn([7, 1, 3, 5, 9]) == 5
    try:
        fn([])
        assert False, "expected ValueError"
    except ValueError:
        pass
    assert fn([2, 2]) == 2

docstring_spec:
Given a non-empty list of ints `values`, return the value that occurs most
frequently. If two or more values tie for the highest frequency, return the
smallest of the tied values. Raise ValueError if `values` is empty. Edge
cases: a single-element list returns that element; a list where all
elements are equal returns that shared value; when several values tie for
the max frequency, the numerically smallest one wins regardless of which
appeared first in the list; negative values participate in the "smallest"
tie-break normally.
hidden_tests:
def check(fn):
    assert fn([1]) == 1
    assert fn([1, 1, 2]) == 1
    assert fn([1, 2, 2, 3, 3]) == 2
    assert fn([5, 3, 5, 3, 1]) == 3
    assert fn([-2, -2, -1, -1, -1]) == -1
    assert fn([9, 9, 9, 1, 1, 1, 2]) == 1
    assert fn([4, 4, 4, 4]) == 4
    try:
        fn([])
        assert False, "expected ValueError"
    except ValueError:
        pass
    assert fn([0, 0, -5, -5, 2]) == -5

docstring_spec:
Given a list of ints `values` and a non-negative int `k`, sort the values
and remove the `k` smallest and `k` largest elements (`2*k` elements total),
then return the sum of what remains. If `2*k >= len(values)`, return 0
(everything is trimmed away, including the case of trimming an empty or
too-small list). `k == 0` returns the sum of the full list unchanged. Raise
ValueError if `k` is negative. Edge cases: an empty list with `k=0` sums to
0; `k` exactly consuming the whole list returns 0; duplicate values are
trimmed by sorted position, not by distinct value (each occurrence counts
separately); negative values are summed normally.
hidden_tests:
def check(fn):
    assert fn([], 0) == 0
    assert fn([5], 0) == 5
    assert fn([5], 1) == 0
    assert fn([1, 2, 3, 4, 5], 0) == 15
    assert fn([1, 2, 3, 4, 5], 1) == 9
    assert fn([1, 2, 3, 4, 5], 2) == 3
    assert fn([5, 1, 4, 2, 3], 1) == 9
    assert fn([1, 1, 1, 1], 1) == 2
    assert fn([-3, -1, 0, 2, 4], 1) == 1
    try:
        fn([1, 2, 3], -1)
        assert False, "expected ValueError"
    except ValueError:
        pass

docstring_spec:
Given a list `values` of at least 2 ints, return the smallest non-negative
absolute difference between any two distinct positions in the list:
`min(|values[i] - values[j]|)` over all `i != j`. This is equivalent to
sorting the list and taking the minimum difference between adjacent sorted
elements. Raise ValueError if `values` has fewer than 2 elements. Edge
cases: exactly two elements returns their absolute difference directly; any
duplicate value appearing twice anywhere in the list forces the result to
0; unsorted input is handled correctly; negative values are handled via
absolute difference after sorting.

Given non-negative ints `a` and `b` with `a <= b`, return the sum of
popcount(n) (the number of set bits, i.e. `bin(n).count('1')`) for every
integer `n` in the inclusive range `[a, b]`. Raise ValueError if `a` is
negative or if `a > b`. Edge cases: `a == b` returns the popcount of that
single value (popcount of 0 is 0); the range `[0, 1]` sums to 1; ranges
crossing a power-of-two boundary are summed correctly; a single power-of-two
value (e.g. 8) contributes exactly 1.

Given a non-negative int `byte_val` in the inclusive range `[0, 255]`
(representing a single byte), return a new int with its high nibble (bits
4-7) and low nibble (bits 0-3) swapped. For example `0x12` (high nibble
`0x1`, low nibble `0x2`) becomes `0x21`. Raise ValueError if `byte_val` is
outside `[0, 255]`. Edge cases: `0` maps to `0`; `0xFF` maps to itself
(both nibbles full and identical); a value with an empty low nibble
(`0xF0`) maps to a value with an empty high nibble (`0x0F`) and vice versa;
single-nibble values like `0x1` and `0x10` swap into each other.

Given a non-negative int `n`, return 1 if the number of set bits in its
binary representation is odd ("odd parity"), else return 0. `n = 0` has
zero set bits and therefore returns 0. Raise ValueError if `n` is negative.
Edge cases: powers of two (exactly one set bit) always return 1; a value
with all bits set in a byte (`0xFF`, eight set bits) returns 0 (even); the
smallest values 0 and 1 are handled as explicit base cases.
hidden_tests:
def check(fn):
    assert fn(0) == 0
    assert fn(1) == 1
    assert fn(2) == 1
    assert fn(3) == 0
    assert fn(7) == 1
    assert fn(8) == 1
    assert fn(255) == 0
    assert fn(256) == 1
    try:
        fn(-1)
        assert False, "expected ValueError"
    except ValueError:
        pass

docstring_spec:
Given a positive int `n` (`n >= 1`), return the value of the lowest
(least significant) set bit -- equivalently `n & (-n)` under two's
complement, or the largest power of two that divides `n`. For example
`lowest_set_bit(12)` (`0b1100`) is `4` (`0b0100`). Raise ValueError if
`n <= 0`. Edge cases: `n = 1` returns `1`; any power of two returns itself;
an odd number always returns `1`; large powers of two (e.g. 1024) return
themselves unchanged.
